skill.name: higgsfield-soul-id
skill.description: Train a Higgsfield Soul Character from a set of face photos for identity-consistent image and video generation. The skill guides photo selection, creates the Soul ID with the Higgsfield CLI, waits for training, and explains how to reuse the ID in generation workflows.
skill.summary: Train reusable Higgsfield Soul IDs for identity-consistent image and video generation.
skill.author: higgsfield-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and reconnaissance patterns. Most are false positives caused by documented Higgsfield CLI commands, but the remote installer is a true positive because it pipes a network script directly into a shell. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The bootstrap step downloads an install script from a GitHub raw URL and pipes it directly to sh. This is a true positive because the command executes remote code without pinning, signature verification, or a review step. It creates supply-chain risk even though the intent appears to be CLI installation rather than malware.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-shell pattern is present and semantically used for installing an external CLI. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, but the execution risk is direct and well established.

medium_findings.title: Broad Bash Execution For External Service Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and instructs the assistant to run multiple Higgsfield CLI commands for account checks, training, waiting, generation, and listing IDs. This is mostly expected for the skill, but it still gives the skill a larger execution surface than a documentation-only workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and fit the stated purpose, which lowers concern. Risk remains because the skill relies on external command execution and account-state operations.

medium_findings.title: Biometric Photo Upload Workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow asks for 5 to 20 face photos and uses the CLI to upload local image paths or existing upload IDs. This appears necessary for Soul training, but it handles sensitive face data and should require clear user consent before submission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file requirements and upload behavior are explicit in the skill text. The privacy concern is semantic rather than malicious because the photos are central to the advertised capability.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static reconnaissance alerts map to user-facing checks such as account status, paid plan status, training timeout guidance, and photo quality guidance. I did not find evidence of host enumeration, credential harvesting, or hidden system discovery in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is visible workflow guidance and troubleshooting content. Confidence is high because the cited lines do not request system inventory or secret collection.

low_findings.title: Inline CLI Examples Triggered Command Detectors
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts are examples of expected Higgsfield commands rather than Ruby backtick execution or dynamic shell construction. They are still relevant as a Bash-backed workflow but do not show command injection by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are static examples with placeholders, not string-built shell execution. The analyzer label mentioning Ruby backticks does not match the Markdown content.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Download Executed By sh
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads an external installer and immediately executes it through the shell. Safer publishing would require a pinned release, checksum verification, or manual review instructions before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly present on one line and there is no adjacent integrity verification. This is a concrete supply-chain execution issue.

content.value_statement: Identity-consistent image and video generation needs a reusable face model, not repeated manual prompts. This skill guides photo selection, Soul ID training, and reuse through the Higgsfield CLI.
actual_capabilities: Guides collection of 5 to 20 suitable face photos for Soul training.
Chooses between image-focused and cinematic Soul variants based on the user goal.
Runs Higgsfield CLI commands to create a Soul ID from local photos or upload IDs.
Waits for Soul training completion and returns a reusable identity reference.
Shows how to use the trained Soul ID in Higgsfield image and video generation.
limitations: Requires the Higgsfield CLI, authentication, and a paid Basic or higher plan.
Requires clear consent and suitable face photos from the person being modeled.
Does not perform one-shot face swaps or train non-photo fictional characters.
Training quality depends on the variety, clarity, and consistency of input photos.

use_cases.title: Creator Avatar Setup
use_cases.description: Train a reusable Soul ID so a creator can appear consistently across generated thumbnails, portraits, and short video concepts.

use_cases.title: Brand Character Production
use_cases.description: Create an identity reference for approved talent so campaign visuals can keep the same face across image and cinematic outputs.

use_cases.title: Personal AI Portrait Workflow
use_cases.description: Prepare photo inputs, train a personal Soul Character, and reuse it later without repeating the setup process.

prompt_templates.title: Train My Soul
prompt_templates.prompt: Train a Higgsfield Soul for me named Alex using these photos: ./photos/alex1.jpg, ./photos/alex2.jpg, ./photos/alex3.jpg, ./photos/alex4.jpg, ./photos/alex5.jpg.

prompt_templates.title: Check Photo Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Soul training photos and tell me which ones to replace before starting the Higgsfield training job.

prompt_templates.title: Create Cinematic Soul
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cinematic Higgsfield Soul for Jordan. Use these uploaded image IDs and prepare it for future video generation.

prompt_templates.title: Train And Reuse Identity
prompt_templates.prompt: Train a Soul ID for Priya, wait for completion, then show me the next Higgsfield generation step for a studio portrait and a cinematic clip.

output_examples.input: User provides a name and six clear face photos.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the photo set, starts Soul training with the Higgsfield CLI, waits silently, and reports that the named Soul is ready.

output_examples.input: User says training failed after uploading group photos and filtered selfies.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies likely photo quality issues and asks for single-person, sharp, unfiltered replacement images before retrying.

output_examples.input: User asks how to use an existing Soul in a new image workflow.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that the existing Soul ID can be passed into a Soul-compatible Higgsfield generation model.

best_practices: Get explicit consent before uploading face photos for identity model training.
Use varied, well-lit, single-person photos with visible eyes and minimal occlusion.
Confirm account status and plan requirements before starting a training job.
anti_patterns: Do not train a Soul from group photos or images where the subject is unclear.
Do not upload another person's face photos without permission.
Do not use this skill for one-shot face swaps or fictional character prompting.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a Higgsfield Soul ID, which is a reusable identity reference for supported image and video generation workflows.

faq.question: How many photos are needed?
faq.answer: The skill asks for at least 5 face photos and supports up to 20. A varied set usually gives better results.

faq.question: Does it need a Higgsfield account?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow requires the Higgsfield CLI, an authenticated account, and a paid Basic or higher plan.

faq.question: Can it train a fictional character?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for photo-based identity training, not named fictional characters or prompt-only avatars.

faq.question: Can it use uploaded image IDs instead of local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill states that local paths and already-uploaded image IDs can be used as image inputs.

faq.question: What security review result applies?
faq.answer: The audit marks the skill high risk because it includes a remote installer piped to a shell and uses external CLI commands.
